votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the prime check and verifies that the result list exactly matches the sorted list of primes less than n, which is equivalent to the Coq specification.

The Python code correctly implements the primality check and verifies that the result list exactly matches the sorted list of primes strictly less than n, which is equivalent to the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly implements the prime check and constructs the expected sorted list of primes up to n, which perfectly matches the Coq specification.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec: is_prime checks primality (p >= 2 and no divisors from 2 to sqrt(p)), and count_up_to_spec verifies that result contains exactly the primes in [2, n) in sorted order. The Coq spec requires n >= 0, result contains exactly primes in [2, n), and result is sorted. The Python implementation generates the expected sorted list of primes in [2, n) and checks equality, which is equivalent to the Coq specification for all concrete inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. The is_prime function checks primality by testing divisors up to sqrt(p), which is equivalent to checking all divisors from 2 to p-1 (since if p has a divisor d >= sqrt(p), it also has a divisor p/d <= sqrt(p)). The count_up_to_spec function checks that n >= 0, that result contains exactly the primes in [2, n), and since it builds the expected list in sorted order and compares for equality, this implicitly ensures the ordering property. The Coq spec requires: (1) n >= 0, (2) result contains exactly primes x where 2 <= x < n, and (3) result is sorted. The Python implementation produces the sorted list of primes in [2, n) and checks equality, which satisfies all three conditions.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. The is_prime function checks primality by testing divisors up to sqrt(p), which is equivalent to the Coq definition that checks all divisors from 2 to p-1 (since if p has a divisor d >= sqrt(p), it also has a divisor p/d <= sqrt(p)). The count_up_to_spec function checks that n >= 0, that result contains exactly the primes in [2, n), and since it builds expected as a sorted list and compares for equality, this implicitly ensures the ordering property required by Coq. The concrete behavior matches on all inputs.
Python returns True iff n>=0 and result equals the increasing list of all primes 2<=x<n. Its is_prime matches the Coq predicate (no divisors in 2..p-1), so concrete results align.

Both define primes as integers >=2 with no divisors in [2,p), and count_up_to_spec enforces result equals the increasing list of primes in [2,n). The Python is_prime (trial up to sqrt) matches the Coq definition on all concrete inputs, and result==expected ensures the same membership and order constraints.

Python is_prime correctly matches the Coq prime predicate, and count_up_to_spec enforces result equals the increasing list of primes in [2,n), which is exactly what the Coq spec (membership + strict increasing) entails.
